denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

Cigna has completed its review of the prior authorization request for continuation of brivaracetam (Briviact) for the treatment of drug-resistant focal epilepsy (G40.019). Your provider's request was received on May 28, 2026 at 10:41 AM, and this determination was completed the same day at 10:44 AM. The request is not approved at this time.

The prior authorization previously issued for brivaracetam (Briviact) has lapsed. Continuation of this medication requires an active prior authorization, and a new authorization request with current clinical information must be submitted before the medication can be approved. Because an active authorization is not on file, continuation is not authorized at this time.

You or your provider may request, at no charge, a copy of the claim file and the specific clinical criteria relied upon. Your treating provider may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a Cigna medical director. If you disagree with this decision, you may submit a written appeal within 180 days of the date of this notice. Please refer to your plan materials for instructions on where to submit your appeal.

Sincerely,
Cigna Prior Authorization Review

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
clinical_context: The member is a 62-year-old man with drug-resistant focal epilepsy arising from a left temporal focus (EEG with left anterior temporal spikes), who achieved a marked reduction in seizure frequency on brivaracetam 100 mg twice daily after breakthrough seizures on levetiracetam (limited by irritability) and lamotrigine. He has been seizure-free for approximately eight months on his current dose, and abrupt interruption of an antiseizure medication risks breakthrough seizures and status epilepticus, with implications for his driving eligibility. His prior authorization lapsed administratively at refill, and his neurologist submitted current clinical information, including the seizure diary and prior medication failures, with the continuation request. The denial notice records that the request was received at 10:41 AM and the determination was completed at 10:44 AM the same day, a three-minute turnaround inconsistent with any individualized clinical review of the submitted records, as required for an adverse determination. The notice also omits the ERISA disclosures of the member's appeal and civil-action rights that apply to his employer-sponsored plan.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Cigna, Prior Authorization, neurology, 56-70, M, continuation_of_care_lapsed); dx G40.019 is neurology; brivaracetam continuation realistic; denial_rationale_seed describes continuation_of_care_lapsed (lapsed PA, new auth required); age 62 in band; not 71+ so null employer_archetype fine.

scenario_realism.reasoning: Drug-resistant focal epilepsy seizure-free on brivaracetam after levetiracetam/lamotrigine is a textbook current picture; lapsed-PA continuation denials on self-funded ERISA plans are routine; the 3-minute same-day turnaround and omitted ERISA disclosures are realistic procedural defects; status-epilepticus risk on abrupt withdrawal is medically sound; no age/dx mismatch.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Attributed to Cigna (Cigna medical director, Cigna Prior Authorization Review) with no wrong-insurer tells, but the purely administrative lapsed-auth rationale cites no Cigna-specific guideline (no Medical Necessity Guidelines/MCG), so it reads as insurer-agnostic boilerplate. Not miscast, just unspecific.
insurer_voice.improvement: A lapsed-auth denial is inherently administrative; could add a Cigna guideline tell, accepted as-is (no wrong-insurer tells).

denial_logic.reasoning: Applies continuation_of_care_lapsed faithfully and with realistic shoddiness: denies purely on the procedural lapse ('not on file'), never engages clinical merit, cites no fabricated guideline; the 3-minute window reinforces the rubber-stamp quality; clearly exploitable on appeal (resubmit current info).

clinical_realism.reasoning: Left temporal focus with anterior temporal spikes is classic localization; levetiracetam (irritability) then lamotrigine then brivaracetam 100 mg BID is correctly sequenced and a valid maintenance dose; brivaracetam as a levetiracetam successor is apt; ~8 months seizure-free plausible; abrupt-withdrawal status epilepticus and driving implications accurate; no invented drugs or impossible courses.

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: Brivaracetam (Briviact) is an FDA-approved SV2A-ligand antiseizure medication indicated for focal (partial-onset) seizures, commonly continued in drug-resistant focal epilepsy; correct category, real drug, brand maps to generic; unambiguous match.

diversity_delta.reasoning: No neighbour combines drug-resistant epilepsy with brivaracetam continuation; the same payer/category/subtype neighbour is RRMS/ocrelizumab (different disease/drug); the shared-dx neighbour (UHC lacosamide add-on) differs in payer, sub_tactic, drug, and trajectory (new add-on vs continuation). Distinct on drug + trajectory; not a near-duplicate.

diversity_statistical.reasoning: Max trigram-Jaccard: corpus=0.179 (nearest=case_189_cigna_priorauth), neighbours=0.0. Thresholds: warn>=0.35, fail>=0.5.

safety_redactor.reasoning: Continuation epilepsy appeal; status-epilepticus risk is a medical-necessity argument, not EOL/life-sustaining withdrawal; no SI/self-harm/child abuse/IPV/minor-gender care; commercial Cigna employer-sponsored plan, no Medicare/Medicaid. No banned topics.

contradiction_hunter.reasoning: Gender (62M), dx (drug-resistant focal epilepsy G40.019), requested brivaracetam continuation, and the 10:41/10:44 timestamps agree across letter and context; the lapsed-PA rationale vs resubmitted current info is the allowed appealable falsehood; letter internally consistent; left-temporal laterality consistent; medication timeline coherent; no fact instability.

llm_tell_detector.reasoning: Standard insurer boilerplate and dense chart-style clinical recitation; no 'As an AI' tics, GPT vocabulary, listicles, or marketing filler; nothing at the >=0.7 conspicuous-tell bar.

overall_tone.reasoning: Cold procedural Cigna register, flat 'not approved at this time', circular administrative reasoning, standard rights boilerplate, no apology/marketing; clinical context plain factual, noting the 3-minute turnaround and ERISA omission as observations, not emotional pleading.

financial_auditor.reasoning: No member out-of-pocket figure or dollar liability; only 'at no charge' for records access; silence on cost is standard; no Medicare/Medicaid conflation (employer-sponsored ERISA plan).

legal_auditor.reasoning: Concrete 180-day appeal window with submission details deferred to plan materials; omits the full ERISA §502(a) civil-action and external-review disclosures (the intended realistic procedural-omission flaw, an attack vector not a defect); no invented statutes; no Medicare framing on this commercial plan.

demographic_validator.reasoning: 62M with drug-resistant focal epilepsy from a left temporal focus is age- and sex-appropriate; medication history carries no gender stereotyping; consistent male pronouns; clean epidemiological match.

scope_guard.reasoning: Cigna in-scope, Prior Authorization, self_funded employer-sponsored commercial (ERISA, no Medicare/Medicaid/TRICARE/VA/marketplace), adult 62, member-facing written appeal/peer-to-peer only (the civil-action reference is a standard ERISA disclosure, not court representation), no live-filing/attorney language.

date_sanity.reasoning: All dates in 2026 and consistent; request received May 28, 2026 10:41 AM, determination same day 10:44 AM (post-dates request; the fast turnaround is a review concern, not a date contradiction); 180-day window sensible; ~8 months seizure-free and prior failures stack plausibly before the denial; no impossible durations.

citation_traceability.reasoning: Cites only generic 'specific clinical criteria relied upon' with no named bulletin/module/edition/page and no fabricated alphanumeric code; the administrative lapse rationale needs no clinical citation; realistically vague without over-specification or hallucination.

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Notice omits ERISA appeal/civil-action disclosures (statutory violation); the 3-minute 10:41-to-10:44 turnaround is inconsistent with required individualized clinical review; the denial rests on an administrative lapse despite the neurologist resubmitting current clinical information; the patient is seizure-free ~8 months and abrupt interruption risks status epilepticus. Only a 180-day window and peer-to-peer defend; easy appeal.
exploitable_weaknesses: Omitted ERISA appeal and civil-action rights disclosures for an employer-sponsored plan
Three-minute turnaround (10:41 to 10:44 AM) inconsistent with required individualized clinical review of submitted records
Denial rests on administrative lapse despite neurologist submitting current clinical information (seizure diary, prior med failures); patient seizure-free ~8 months meets standard of care
strong_defenses: 180-day appeal window stated and peer-to-peer review offered
